title: Sicilian cannoli
subtitle: Crisp fried pastry tubes with sweetened ricotta filling
excerpt: Twelve crisp pastry shells deep-fried around metal tubes, filled once cooled with a sweetened ricotta cream studded with dark chocolate and pistachios. The shells can be fried ahead; fill them within an hour of serving so they stay crisp.
sourceNotes: Sicilian cannoli are documented from at least the medieval period, with recipes in Sicilian and southern Italian cookery texts from the nineteenth century onwards. The method appears in pre-1928 Italian regional cookery writing and Artusi, La Scienza in Cucina e l'Arte di Mangiar Bene (1891, public domain), which gives the fried pastry tube and ricotta filling as a recognised Sicilian form. Cross-referenced with early-twentieth-century Sicilian household cookery manuscripts available through Italian library digital archives.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Shells can be fried up to 2 days ahead and stored in an airtight tin at room temperature. Fill within 1 hour of serving.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: Deep-fried at 180 to 185°C. Use a sugar thermometer or instant-read thermometer. Too hot and the shells bubble and burn; too cool and they absorb oil.
baking.decoratingTechnique: dip the filled ends in chopped pistachios

glossaryTerms.definition: The pastry for a cannoli shell contains wine vinegar or Marsala, which inhibits gluten development and produces a thin, tender dough that blisters lightly when fried. The dough is rolled very thin, cut into rounds or squares, and wrapped around metal tubes 11 to 12 cm long. The edge is sealed with a little beaten egg before frying to stop the tube unwinding. Once fried and cooled, the metal tubes are twisted gently and slid out, leaving hollow crisp cylinders ready for filling.

content.text: Sicilian cannoli are made in two stages: the

and the ricotta filling. The shells wrap around metal tubes before frying; the tubes are removed once the pastry cools. Twelve cannoli. You will need metal cannoli tubes or improvise with clean metal cylinders about 11 cm long and 2 cm in diameter. The shells can be made ahead; fill them close to serving.

items.prepNote: well drained overnight in a sieve

content.text: Rub the butter into the flour and sugar until it resembles fine breadcrumbs. Add the egg, Marsala, vinegar, and salt. Mix to a firm, smooth dough; add a drop more Marsala if too dry. Wrap in cling film and rest at room temperature for 30 minutes.

Drain the ricotta in a fine sieve over a bowl in the fridge for at least an hour, or overnight. Beat the drained ricotta, icing sugar, and vanilla together until smooth. Fold in the chopped chocolate. Refrigerate until needed.

Roll the pastry as thin as possible: 1 to 2 mm. Cut into circles or squares roughly 10 to 11 cm across. Wrap each piece around a lightly oiled metal tube, overlapping the edge and pressing firmly with beaten egg to seal. Heat the oil in a large pan to 180 to 185°C. Fry 2 or 3 shells at a time for 2 to 3 minutes, turning once, until golden and blistered. Lift out, cool on paper, then twist and slide out the tubes while still slightly warm. Shells keep crisp in an airtight tin for 2 days.
Within an hour of serving, pipe the ricotta filling into the shells from both ends. Press the exposed ends into the chopped pistachios and dust lightly with icing sugar. Serve immediately.
